author: Alkarim Billawala / alkarim.billawala.ca
weeks: Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology

questions.stem: A 52-year-old with diabetic nephropathy (baseline creatinine 210) has profound weakness after a gastroenteritis during which he kept taking ramipril. K is 7.6; ECG shows peaked T waves and widened QRS. Most appropriate FIRST step?
questions.explain: With hyperkalemic ECG changes (widened QRS), the immediate life-saving step is to stabilize the myocardium with IV calcium gluconate (raises threshold potential; doesn't lower K). Insulin/glucose, furosemide, and dialysis follow.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old mechanic is found confused in his garage. He has a high anion-gap metabolic acidosis, an osmolar gap of 38, and envelope-shaped urine crystals. Which toxic metabolite explains his renal findings?
questions.explain: Garage setting + high osmolar/anion gaps + envelope-shaped (calcium-oxalate) crystals = ethylene glycol. It is metabolized to oxalic acid, which precipitates calcium-oxalate in the tubules → acute kidney injury. Formic acid is the methanol metabolite (blindness).

questions.stem: A 71-year-old on high-dose furosemide infusion for heart failure develops palpitations on day 3; ECG shows flattened T waves and prominent U waves. By what mechanism is the diuretic causing this?
options: Inhibits Na-Cl cotransporter in the DCT
Inhibits Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the ascending loop
Antagonizes aldosterone in the collecting duct
Inhibits carbonic anhydrase in the PCT
questions.explain: Furosemide blocks the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the thick ascending limb, causing urinary potassium wasting → hypokalemia (flattened T waves, U waves). The DCT Na-Cl description is thiazides; aldosterone antagonism is K-sparing.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old with advanced dementia, dependent for hydration, is progressively unresponsive with dry mucous membranes and Na 158. What is the primary derangement?
options: Absolute excess of total body sodium
Relative deficit of total body water
questions.explain: Hypernatremia is a concentration problem from a relative water deficit. Thirst normally prevents it, so it occurs in those who can't access/drink water (dementia, infants, bed-bound). Replace water (oral/NG or D5W).

questions.stem: A 64-year-old with a few weeks of weak stream and nocturia is offered a drug that relaxes bladder-neck and prostate smooth muscle for rapid relief. Which adverse effect should he be counseled about?
questions.explain: This is an α1-blocker (e.g. tamsulosin). Because α1 receptors also control vascular tone, the key adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension/dizziness (also retrograde ejaculation). Gynecomastia is a 5-ARI effect.

questions.stem: A woman on amlodipine and HCTZ has suboptimal BP; the physician considers adding spironolactone, but her K is 5.3. Why is spironolactone contraindicated here?
options: It will dangerously lower her sodium
It antagonizes aldosterone, decreasing distal potassium secretion
questions.explain: Spironolactone blocks the mineralocorticoid receptor → less ENaC/ROMK-mediated potassium secretion → potassium rises. In a patient already hyperkalemic (5.3) this is contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old rationing insulin presents with Kussmaul breathing, pH 7.15, HCO₃ 12, ketonuria, and K 5.8. What explains the hyperkalemia on presentation?
options: Shift of potassium out of cells from insulin deficiency and acidemia
questions.explain: In DKA, insulin deficiency reduces Na/K-ATPase activity and acidemia drives K out of cells (H+/K+ exchange) → transient hyperkalemia despite total-body potassium depletion (which is unmasked once insulin is given).

questions.stem: A 68-year-old with overactive bladder is to be prescribed oxybutynin. Which pre-existing condition is an absolute contraindication?
questions.explain: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic; anticholinergic mydriasis can precipitate an acute attack in narrow-angle glaucoma — an absolute contraindication. (Renal artery stenosis matters for ACEi/ARBs.)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old with 3 days of severe watery diarrhea has HR 115, BP 95/60 (80/50 standing), undetectable JVP. Which lab set fits best?
options: High urine Na, high urine Cl, metabolic alkalosis
Low urine Na, low urine Cl, normal acid-base
Low urine Na, low urine Cl, non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
High urine Na, low urine Cl, high-anion-gap acidosis
questions.explain: Hypovolemia activates RAAS → kidneys avidly reabsorb sodium and chloride (low urine Na/Cl). Lower-GI (diarrheal) fluid is bicarbonate-rich, so its loss causes a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis.

questions.stem: A patient with central diabetes insipidus takes a vasopressin agonist to reduce urine volume. Where in the nephron does it act?
questions.explain: Vasopressin/ADH (and desmopressin) act on V2 receptors in the collecting duct, inserting aquaporin-2 channels to reabsorb water.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old on 3 weeks of furosemide has weakness, orthostatic hypotension, JVP 1 cm, Na 126, K 2.8, HCO₃ 34. What is the most appropriate primary step to correct her hyponatremia?
questions.explain: This is hypovolemic hyponatremia from diuretic-induced volume loss driving non-osmotic ADH. Isotonic saline restores volume, shuts off ADH, and lets the kidney excrete the retained free water. Hypertonic saline is only for acute severe neurologic symptoms (seizures).

questions.stem: A T1DM patient vomiting for 3 days has pH 7.27, pCO₂ 26, Na 132, Cl 83, HCO₃ 10, glucose 19, urea 7, measured osm 290. What is the complete acid-base diagnosis?
options: Anion-gap metabolic acidosis + concurrent metabolic alkalosis
questions.explain: AG = 132−(83+10)=39 (high → AGMA from DKA). Winter's: expected pCO₂ ≈1.5(10)+8=23±2 ~ matches (appropriate compensation). Osmolar gap 0. Delta-delta: ΔAG=27 but HCO₃ only fell 14 → more base than expected → a concurrent metabolic alkalosis (from vomiting).

questions.stem: A patient on lithium for bipolar disorder is started on hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension and develops a tremor, confusion, and a high lithium level. What is the mechanism?
options: Thiazide reduces proximal lithium clearance, raising serum lithium
questions.explain: Thiazides cause volume contraction and increased proximal sodium (and lithium) reabsorption, raising serum lithium to toxic levels. Monitor levels closely or avoid the combination.

questions.stem: Which single factor is required for the kidney to excrete free water and prevent hyponatremia?
questions.explain: Free-water excretion requires adequate GFR, urinary solute, and crucially suppressed ADH. Inappropriate ADH (SIADH or non-osmotic stimuli) prevents water excretion → hyponatremia.

questions.stem: A man with BPH is started on finasteride. Which counseling point is correct?
options: It shrinks the prostate over ~6 months and lowers PSA
It is safe for pregnant partners to handle crushed tablets
questions.explain: Finasteride (5-α reductase inhibitor) blocks testosterone→DHT, shrinking the prostate over ~6 months and roughly halving PSA. It is teratogenic (pregnant caregivers must not handle crushed/split tablets); α1-blockers give the fast relief.

questions.stem: A man with erectile dysfunction and stable angina on nitrates asks about sildenafil. What is the key issue?
options: It is absolutely contraindicated with nitrates (severe hypotension)
questions.explain: PDE5 inhibitors plus nitrates cause refractory, life-threatening hypotension (additive cGMP-mediated vasodilation) — an absolute contraindication.

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure has a serum sodium of 130 and obvious peripheral edema. What does the low sodium tell you about his ECF volume?
options: Nothing directly — sodium concentration doesn't predict ECF volume
He has too little total body sodium
questions.explain: Sodium concentration reflects water balance, not volume. This patient is hypervolemic (edema, high total-body sodium) yet hyponatremic because RAAS/ADH retain even more water. Concentration ≠ volume.

questions.stem: Which diuretic acts at the proximal tubule, promotes bicarbonate wasting, and can cause a metabolic acidosis?
questions.explain: Acetazolamide inhibits carbonic anhydrase in the proximal tubule, blocking NaHCO₃ reabsorption → bicarbonate wasting and a metabolic acidosis (used for glaucoma, altitude sickness, and metabolic alkalosis).

questions.stem: A patient's hypokalemia (K 2.9) fails to correct despite repeated KCl. Which electrolyte must be checked and replaced?
questions.explain: Hypomagnesemia increases renal potassium wasting (via ROMK) and makes hypokalemia refractory — potassium won't correct until magnesium is replaced.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic hyponatremia (Na 116 for days) is asymptomatic. Why must correction be slow?
options: Rapid overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome
Sodium can only be given orally
questions.explain: In chronic hyponatremia the brain has adapted; overly rapid correction causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis) — permanent injury. Limit the daily rise. (Acute symptomatic hyponatremia is the scenario that justifies faster, hypertonic correction.)

cards.prompt: What do sodium content vs sodium concentration each determine?
cards.answer: Sodium content determines ECF volume (edema vs hypovolemia); sodium concentration determines intracellular volume (cell swelling/shrinkage). Concentration does NOT predict ECF volume.

cards.prompt: Which systems control sodium balance vs water balance?
cards.answer: RAAS controls sodium (volume) — renin from low BP/low NaCl delivery. Thirst and ADH control water (concentration); ADH acts on the collecting duct via aquaporin-2.

cards.prompt: Why does hyponatremia occur, mechanistically?
cards.answer: Relative water excess — free-water excretion fails from low GFR, low urinary solute, or (usually) inappropriate ADH activity.

cards.prompt: How fast should chronic vs acute symptomatic hyponatremia be corrected?
cards.answer: Acute/symptomatic (seizures) → hypertonic 3% saline for rapid partial correction. Chronic → correct slowly; overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome.

cards.prompt: Why is hypernatremia uncommon, and who gets it?
cards.answer: Thirst is a powerful defense, so it occurs mainly in those who can't access/drink water (infants, elderly, dementia, coma) or with large free-water losses (DI, osmotic diuresis).

cards.prompt: Contrast ECF volume expansion vs depletion and their treatments.
cards.answer: Expansion (heart failure): ↑JVP, edema, crackles → restrict Na, diurese. Depletion (diarrhea): tachycardia, ↓JVP, low urine Na/Cl → isotonic saline resuscitation.

cards.prompt: Where is body potassium and what maintains the gradient?
cards.answer: 98% is intracellular (~150 in vs ~4 out); the Na/K-ATPase maintains the gradient that sets the resting membrane potential for cardiac/nerve/muscle function.

cards.prompt: How does aldosterone drive potassium excretion?
cards.answer: It inserts ENaC; sodium entry makes the lumen electronegative, which drives potassium secretion via ROMK channels in the distal nephron.

cards.prompt: What are the three mechanisms of hypokalemia?
cards.answer: Shift into cells (insulin, β2-agonists, alkalemia, refeeding), GI loss (diarrhea, laxatives), and renal loss (diuretics, hyperaldosteronism, Bartter/Gitelman).

cards.prompt: What ECG changes occur in hypokalemia?
cards.answer: Flattened T waves, prominent U waves, ST depression, and prolonged QT predisposing to ventricular arrhythmias.

cards.prompt: Outline the four steps of hyperkalemia management.
cards.answer: Stabilize (IV calcium — doesn't lower K), Shift (insulin+glucose, salbutamol, ± bicarbonate), Excrete (saline+furosemide or a K binder), Dialyze (if refractory/anuric).

cards.prompt: Why give calcium in hyperkalemia if it doesn't lower potassium?
cards.answer: It raises the threshold potential, restoring the safety margin and stabilizing the myocardium to prevent fatal arrhythmia while other measures lower the potassium.

cards.prompt: How are the anion gap and osmolar gap calculated?
cards.answer: Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO₃) (normal ~12). Osmolar gap = measured − calculated osmolality [2(Na)+glucose+urea] (normal <10; high suggests toxic alcohols).

cards.prompt: Contrast ethylene glycol and methanol toxicity and treatment.
cards.answer: Ethylene glycol → oxalic acid → calcium-oxalate crystals → renal failure. Methanol → formic acid → optic-nerve damage/blindness. Treat both with fomepizole (block alcohol dehydrogenase) + hemodialysis.

cards.prompt: Match the diuretic class to its tubular site.
cards.answer: Acetazolamide → PCT; loop (furosemide) → thick ascending limb (Na-K-2Cl); thiazide → DCT (Na-Cl); amiloride/MRA → collecting duct (ENaC/aldosterone).

cards.prompt: Compare the two BPH drug classes.
cards.answer: α1-blockers (tamsulosin) relax smooth muscle for rapid relief (orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation). 5-α reductase inhibitors (finasteride) shrink the prostate over ~6 months (lower PSA, teratogenic).

cards.prompt: ADH acts on which nephron segment?
A seizing patient with acute hyponatremia is treated with:
Hypernatremia from water deficit is corrected with:
The earliest ECG change of hyperkalemia is:
Loop diuretics inhibit which transporter?
Compared with loop diuretics, thiazides affect calcium how?
The main risk of spironolactone/amiloride is:
The gold-standard antidote that blocks alcohol dehydrogenase is:
Oxybutynin is contraindicated in:
PDE5 inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated with:
Select causes of hyperkalemia:
Select non-osmotic triggers of ADH release:
Select adverse effects of loop diuretics:
Select causes of hypokalemia:
Select high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis causes:

cards.text: Anion gap = Na − ({{Cl}} + {{HCO3}}); normal is about {{12}}.
cards.explain: Adjust for albumin (low albumin lowers the apparent gap).

cards.text: Calculated osmolality = 2(Na) + {{glucose}} + {{urea}}; a gap > {{10}} suggests a toxic alcohol.
cards.explain: A rapid screen while specific levels are pending.
Calcium protects the heart but does not lower potassium.

cards.text: About {{98}}% of body potassium is intracellular, maintained by the {{Na/K-ATPase}} pump.
cards.explain: This gradient sets the resting membrane potential.

cards.text: Loop diuretics act on the {{thick ascending limb}}; thiazides on the {{distal convoluted tubule}}.
cards.explain: Loops block Na-K-2Cl; thiazides block Na-Cl.

cards.prompt: Order the management of severe hyperkalemia:
items: Insulin + glucose (± salbutamol) to shift K into cells

cards.prompt: Order the acid-base interpretation steps:
items: Identify the primary process (pCO2 vs HCO3)
Calculate the anion gap (± osmolar gap)
Apply delta-delta to find hidden disorders

cards.prompt: Order the management of suspected toxic-alcohol ingestion:
items: Recognize high anion-gap acidosis + high osmolar gap
Hemodialysis to clear the toxin + correct acidosis
Continue until levels and acidosis resolve

cards.prompt: Match the diuretic to its site/mechanism:
Match the urologic drug to its use:
Match the toxic alcohol to its injury:
Match the disturbance to its ECG change:
pairs: Peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave

cards.prompt: How does isotonic saline correct hypovolemic hyponatremia?
cards.answer: It restores effective circulating volume, switching off the non-osmotic ADH drive, so the kidney can excrete the retained free water and the sodium rises.

cards.prompt: What distinguishes central from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus?
cards.answer: Central (AVP-deficient) lacks ADH and responds to desmopressin; nephrogenic (AVP-resistant) has ADH but unresponsive kidneys (lithium, hypercalcemia) and doesn't respond to desmopressin.

cards.prompt: Why does severe diarrhea cause a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis?
cards.answer: Lower-GI fluid is bicarbonate-rich, so its loss depletes bicarbonate without adding unmeasured anions — a hyperchloremic, normal-gap acidosis.

cards.prompt: Why does hypomagnesemia cause refractory hypokalemia?
cards.answer: Low magnesium increases ROMK-mediated renal potassium secretion, so potassium keeps being lost until magnesium is replaced.

cards.prompt: Why do α1-blockers cause retrograde ejaculation?
cards.answer: α1A receptors in the vas deferens/seminal vesicles/bladder neck normally close the bladder neck during ejaculation; blocking them lets semen flow backward into the bladder.

cards.prompt: What are the uses and key effect of acetazolamide?
cards.answer: It inhibits carbonic anhydrase in the PCT (bicarbonate wasting → metabolic acidosis); used for glaucoma, altitude sickness, and to treat a metabolic alkalosis.

cards.prompt: How does mirabegron treat overactive bladder, and its main caution?
cards.answer: It is a β3-adrenergic agonist that relaxes the detrusor to increase bladder capacity; watch for hypertension and tachycardia (a non-anticholinergic alternative useful when antimuscarinics are contraindicated).

guides.title: Week 34 · 1 Sodium & Water Physiology
guides.html:
Core principle

Sodium & Water Physiology

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The single idea that unlocks every sodium problem — content vs concentration — and the RAAS/ADH systems that defend each.

The one principle

Content vs concentration
Sodium CONTENT determines extracellular fluid (ECF) volume (edema vs hypovolemia). Sodium CONCENTRATION determines intracellular volume (cell swelling vs shrinkage). The serum sodium concentration does NOT tell you the ECF volume — a patient can be hyponatremic and hypervolemic, euvolemic, or hypovolemic.

Two separate control systems

SystemSensesEffector
Sodium balance (volume)Blood pressure / effective circulating volume (baroreceptors, macula densa)RAAS — low BP/low NaCl delivery → renin → angiotensin II + aldosterone → sodium retention
Water balance (concentration)Osmolality (and non-osmotic triggers)Thirst (intake) and ADH/vasopressin (output)

ADH (vasopressin)

  • Released by hyperosmolality, profound ECF depletion, and non-osmotic stimuli (nausea, pain, drugs).
  • Acts on the collecting duct → inserts aquaporin-2 channels → water reabsorption (concentrated urine).
Sodium content = volume; sodium concentration = water balance. RAAS defends volume; thirst + ADH defend concentration. ADH works at the collecting duct via aquaporin-2 — the key to DI and SIADH.

guides.title: Week 34 · 2 Hyponatremia
guides.html:
Too much water

Hyponatremia

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why hyponatremia is always a water problem, the ADH triggers behind it, and the acute-vs-chronic correction rules.

It's always relative water excess

Hyponatremia (Na <135) reflects relative water retention. Excreting free water requires GFR, urinary solute, and switched-off ADH — so hyponatremia means one of those failed: low GFR, low solute, or (usually) inappropriate ADH.

ADH triggers to hunt for

  • Non-osmotic ADH: true/effective volume depletion (the body chooses BP over osmolality), pain, nausea/vomiting, intracranial/intrathoracic disease, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency.
  • Drugs: thiazides, NSAIDs, SSRIs, carbamazepine, chemotherapy.

Presentation

A rapid fall causes cellular (cerebral) swelling → headache, confusion, seizures, coma. Chronic hyponatremia is better tolerated (brain adapts).

Correct at the right speed
Acute / severely symptomatic (seizures): hypertonic (3%) saline for rapid partial correction to relieve cerebral edema. Chronic (>48 h) or severe (<120): correct slowly (limit the daily rise) — overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (permanent brain injury). Hypovolemic hyponatremia: isotonic saline restores volume and shuts off the non-osmotic ADH drive.
Hyponatremia = too much water (usually inappropriate ADH). Seizing → 3% saline; chronic → correct slowly (ODS risk). Hypovolemic hyponatremia → isotonic saline turns off ADH and the kidney dumps the free water.

guides.title: Week 34 · 3 Hypernatremia & ECF Volume Disorders
guides.html:
Too little water

Hypernatremia & ECF Volume Disorders

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why hypernatremia means a water deficit (and who can't drink), and how to read ECF volume extremes.

Hypernatremia = relative water deficit

Na >145 implies too little water. Thirst is such a powerful defense that hypernatremia is uncommon unless the patient can't access/drink water: infants, the elderly, dementia, coma, stroke. Exacerbated by free-water losses — diabetes insipidus (central/AVP-deficient or nephrogenic/AVP-resistant) and osmotic diuresis (hyperglycemia).

  • Presentation: brain-cell shrinkage → confusion, decreased consciousness (and risk of intracranial bleeding with rapid shifts).
  • Management: replace water (oral/NG free water or IV D5W); correct at a controlled rate to avoid cerebral edema.

ECF volume extremes (a sodium-content problem)

Volume expansion (e.g. heart failure)Volume depletion (e.g. diarrhea)
DriverLow effective circulating volume → RAAS retains NaTrue fluid/solute loss
SignsWeight gain, ↑JVP, crackles, bilateral edemaTachycardia, hypotension, ↓JVP, poor turgor
UrineLow urine Na (avid retention)Low urine Na/Cl (RAAS-driven reabsorption)
TreatRestrict Na, diurese, improve cardiac functionIsotonic (normal) saline resuscitation
Hypernatremia = water deficit in someone who can't drink (or has DI). ECF volume is a sodium-content story: heart failure retains Na (edema) → diurese; diarrhea loses volume (low urine Na/Cl) → normal saline.

guides.title: Week 34 · 4 Potassium Physiology & Hypokalemia
guides.html:
Low K

Potassium Physiology & Hypokalemia

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why 98% of potassium hides in cells, how aldosterone sets renal excretion, and the three buckets of hypokalemia.

Why potassium matters

98% of body potassium is intracellular (~150 mmol/L in vs ~4 out); the Na/K-ATPase maintains the gradient that sets the resting membrane potential for cardiac, nerve, and muscle function. Small serum changes have big electrical effects.

Renal handling

90% of filtered K is reabsorbed proximally; final excretion is set in the distal nephron. Aldosterone inserts ENaC → Na enters the principal cell → lumen-negative voltage → K secreted via ROMK.

Hypokalemia (K <3.5) — three buckets

BucketCauses
Shift into cellsInsulin, β2-agonists (salbutamol), alkalemia, refeeding, hypothermia
GI lossDiarrhea, laxative abuse (lower-GI fluid is K-rich)
Renal lossLoop/thiazide diuretics, hyperaldosteronism, Bartter/Gitelman
  • Presentation: weakness, hyporeflexia, palpitations. ECG: flattened T waves, prominent U waves, ST depression, long QT → ventricular arrhythmia.
  • Replace: oral KCl preferred; IV KCl for severe/NPO with cardiac monitoring and rate limits. Always check and replace magnesium (low Mg makes hypokalemia refractory).
K is mostly intracellular; aldosterone (ENaC→ROMK) drives renal excretion. Hypokalemia = shift, GI loss, or renal loss → flattened T + U waves. Replace KCl (oral preferred) and fix magnesium.

guides.title: Week 34 · 5 Hyperkalemia
guides.html:
High K

Hyperkalemia

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The causes, the lethal ECG march, and the four-step stabilize-shift-excrete-dialyze algorithm.

Causes (three buckets)

BucketCauses
Shift out of cellsInsulin deficiency (DKA), acidemia, cell lysis (rhabdomyolysis, tumour lysis, hemolysis, burns)
Decreased excretionAKI, advanced CKD, low aldosterone effect (ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, NSAIDs)
Increased intakeRarely the sole cause unless renal function is poor

The lethal ECG march

Peaked, narrow T waves → flattening P wave + PR prolongation → widening QRS → sine wave → asystole/VF. ECG changes (not just the number) drive urgency.

Four sequential steps
1. Stabilize — IV calcium gluconate (raises the threshold potential; does NOT lower potassium — it buys time). 2. Shift — IV insulin + glucose, inhaled salbutamol, ± sodium bicarbonate (if acidotic). 3. Excrete — IV saline + furosemide, or a gut potassium binder (patiromer). 4. Dialyze — definitive removal if anuric, refractory, or severe CKD.

Two practical points

  • Pseudohyperkalemia: a hemolyzed sample, a tight tourniquet/fist-clenching, or marked thrombocytosis/leukocytosis can falsely elevate potassium — if there are no ECG changes and the result is unexpected, repeat the sample (whole-blood/blood-gas) before aggressive treatment.
  • After the acute treatment: recheck potassium and glucose (insulin can cause delayed hypoglycemia), look for and stop the cause (hold ACEi/ARB/spironolactone/NSAIDs, treat AKI), and remember the shift measures are temporary — potassium must still be removed (binder, diuresis, or dialysis).
Hyperkalemia = shift out, decreased excretion (RAAS blockers!), or intake. Treat by the ECG: calcium to stabilize (doesn't lower K), insulin/glucose + salbutamol to shift, furosemide/binder to excrete, dialysis if refractory. Confirm an unexpected value (pseudohyperkalemia) and always remove the potassium after shifting it.

guides.title: Week 34 · 6 Acid-Base & Toxic Alcohols
guides.html:
Gaps

Acid-Base & Toxic Alcohols

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The anion gap and osmolar gap, the compensation check, and the toxic alcohols whose metabolites blind and poison.

The gaps

  • Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO₃); normal ~12 (adjust for albumin). High-AG causes: GOLDMARK / MUDPILES (glycols, lactate, methanol, aspirin, renal failure, ketoacidosis…).
  • Osmolar gap = measured − calculated osmolality [calc = 2(Na) + glucose + urea]; normal <10. A high osmolar gap is a rapid screen for toxic alcohols.
  • Compensation (Winter's): expected pCO₂ = 1.5 × HCO₃ + 8 ± 2. Delta-delta: ΔAG should ≈ ΔHCO₃ — a mismatch reveals a concurrent metabolic alkalosis (e.g. DKA + vomiting) or non-AG acidosis.

Toxic alcohols — the metabolite is the poison

AlcoholToxic metabolite → injury
Ethylene glycol (antifreeze)Glycolic acid (acidosis) + oxalic acid → calcium-oxalate crystals → acute renal failure
Methanol (windshield fluid)Formic acid → optic nerve/retina → blindness; basal-ganglia necrosis
Don't wait for levels
A high osmolar gap + high-AG acidosis is enough to act. Block alcohol dehydrogenase with fomepizole (gold standard; ethanol infusion is the alternative) to stop metabolite formation, and use hemodialysis to clear the small, water-soluble toxin and correct the acidosis. Waiting for confirmatory levels risks blindness or death.
High anion gap → GOLDMARK; add an osmolar gap to catch toxic alcohols. Ethylene glycol → oxalate crystals + renal failure; methanol → blindness. Treat with fomepizole + dialysis before levels return.

guides.title: Week 34 · 7 Diuretics
guides.html:
By the nephron

Diuretics

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Each diuretic class by its site, mechanism, and the electrolyte derangement it predictably causes.

Diuretics along the nephron

ClassSite / mechanismKey adverse effects
Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide)PCT — blocks NaHCO₃ reabsorptionMetabolic acidosis, hypokalemia (glaucoma, altitude)
Loop (furosemide, bumetanide)Thick ascending limb — blocks Na-K-2ClHypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, hyperuricemia, ototoxicity, contraction alkalosis; contraindicated in anuria
Thiazide-like (HCTZ, chlorthalidone)DCT — blocks Na-ClHyponatremia, hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, hypercalcemia; raises lithium levels
K-sparing / ENaC blocker (amiloride, triamterene)Collecting duct — blocks ENaCHyperkalemia
Aldosterone antagonist (spironolactone, eplerenone)Collecting duct — blocks mineralocorticoid receptorHyperkalemia, gynecomastia (spironolactone); avoid in hyperkalemia/Addison's
Two recurring traps
Loops waste calcium; thiazides retain it ("loops lose calcium"). Thiazides raise lithium to toxic levels (reduced proximal lithium clearance). Both loops and thiazides cause hypokalemia and hyperuricemia (gout flares).
CA inhibitor → PCT (acidosis); loop → Na-K-2Cl (loses K/Mg/Ca, ototoxic); thiazide → Na-Cl (low Na, high Ca, lithium toxicity); K-sparing/MRA → collecting duct (hyperkalemia). NSAIDs blunt loop efficacy.

guides.title: Week 34 · 8 Urologic Pharmacology
guides.html:
Bladder & prostate

Urologic Pharmacology

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The drugs for overactive bladder, BPH, and erectile dysfunction — by receptor, with their signature cautions.

Overactive bladder

ClassMechanismCaution
Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, solifenacin)M3 antagonism → relax detrusorDry mouth, constipation, confusion (elderly); contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma
β3-agonist (mirabegron)Relaxes detrusor via β3 receptorsHypertension, tachycardia

Benign prostatic hyperplasia

ClassMechanism / effectCaution
α1-blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin; doxazosin non-selective)Relax prostate/bladder-neck smooth muscle → rapid symptom reliefFirst-dose orthostatic hypotension (compounds other antihypertensives), retrograde ("dry") ejaculation
5-α reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride)Block testosterone → DHT → shrinks the prostate over ~6 monthsImpotence, ↓libido, gynecomastia; lowers PSA; teratogenic (pregnant caregivers shouldn't handle)

The two BPH classes are complementary (immediate dynamic relief + long-term size reduction) and are often combined.

Erectile dysfunction

PDE5 inhibitors + nitrates = danger
PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) block cGMP breakdown → vasodilation. AEs: flushing, headache, hypotension, priapism, blue-tinted vision (PDE6). Absolutely contraindicated with nitrates (refractory, life-threatening hypotension).
OAB: antimuscarinics (avoid in glaucoma) or mirabegron. BPH: α1-blocker for fast relief (orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation) + 5-ARI to shrink the gland (teratogen, lowers PSA). PDE5 inhibitor + nitrate = forbidden.

guides.title: Week 34 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology

Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Hyperkalemia with ECG changes → IV calcium FIRST (stabilize), then insulin/glucose + salbutamol (shift), then excrete/dialyze
Acute symptomatic hyponatremia / seizures → hypertonic (3%) saline; chronic → correct slowly (osmotic demyelination risk)
High anion-gap acidosis + high osmolar gap → toxic alcohol — fomepizole + dialysis, don't wait for levels
Ethylene glycol → oxalate crystals + renal failure; methanol → blindness
PDE5 inhibitor + nitrate → life-threatening hypotension (absolute contraindication)
Refractory hypokalemia → check and replace magnesium

Sodium & water Na/water

Content = ECF volume (edema/hypovolemia)
Concentration = cell volume (does NOT predict ECF volume)
RAAS defends volume (sodium)
Thirst + ADH defend concentration (water)
ADH site collecting duct → aquaporin-2

Dysnatremias Na disorders

Hyponatremia water excess (inappropriate ADH)
Acute/seizing hypertonic 3% saline
Chronic correct slowly → avoid ODS
Hypovolemic hypoNa isotonic saline shuts off ADH
Hypernatremia water deficit (can't drink / DI) → free water/D5W

Potassium K+

98% intracellular Na/K-ATPase gradient
Aldosterone ENaC → ROMK → K secretion
Hypokalemia shift / GI loss / renal loss; U waves
Refractory hypoK replace magnesium
Hyperkalemia shift out / ↓excretion (RAAS blockers)
HyperK steps stabilize → shift → excrete → dialyze

Acid-base Acid-base

Anion gap Na−(Cl+HCO3); ~12; GOLDMARK
Osmolar gap >10 → toxic alcohol
Winter's expected pCO2 = 1.5(HCO3)+8±2
Delta-delta ΔAG vs ΔHCO3 → hidden disorder
Ethylene glycol oxalate → renal failure
Methanol formate → blindness
Antidote fomepizole + dialysis

Diuretics Diuretics

Acetazolamide PCT; acidosis
Loop TAL Na-K-2Cl; loses K/Mg/Ca, ototoxic
Thiazide DCT Na-Cl; low Na, high Ca, ↑lithium
K-sparing/MRA collecting duct; hyperkalemia
Loops vs thiazides loops lose calcium, thiazides keep it

Urologic Rx Urology Rx

OAB antimuscarinic oxybutynin — avoid in glaucoma
OAB β3 mirabegron — HTN/tachycardia
BPH α1-blocker tamsulosin — orthostatic, retrograde ejaculation
BPH 5-ARI finasteride — shrinks prostate, ↓PSA, teratogen
ED PDE5 sildenafil — no nitrates
Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.mechanism: Loop diuretic — inhibits Na-K-2Cl in the thick ascending limb
facets.adverse: Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, hyperuricemia, ototoxicity, contraction alkalosis

ref.week: Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology
Week 34 · KU II — Fluids, Electrolytes & Renal Pharmacology

facets.mechanism: Thiazide — inhibits Na-Cl in the DCT
facets.adverse: Hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, hypercalcemia; raises lithium levels
